feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex and End Point
description: The controller implements a dual-mode PCIe core that can operate as either a Root Complex (RC) or an End Point (EP). Default mode is selected by PCIESSMODE[1:0] bootstrap pins, and software can override the selection. In either mode the Transaction, Data Link, and MAC layers are integrated in the core. Mode selection impacts configuration header type (Type 1 for RC, Type 0 for EP) and accessible feature set. Proper mode selection must be completed before enabling the LTSSM for link training.
evidence: Section 1.4.1 PCI Express Core Module; BIST_HEADER[HDR_TYPE] (Sections 3.3.2, 3.4.2)
dependencies: PCIESSMODE[1:0] bootstrap; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
Remarks: Choose mode early in boot and avoid changing it after link is up; re-initialize if mode is changed.

feature_name: Link width x1/x2 and Gen1/Gen2 speeds
description: The controller supports a single bidirectional link with up to x2 lane width. It operates at 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2) raw speeds with dynamic width conversion. Speed changes to Gen2 are directed via port logic after initial Gen1 link-up. Lane enable and target speed registers control width and rate. Automatic lane reversal and polarity inversion help with board routing constraints.
evidence: Features (Section 1.3); LINK_CAP[MAX_LINK_SPEED, MAX_LINK_WIDTH] (3.7.5); PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD, LN_EN, NUM_FTS] (3.9.13)
dependencies: SerDes PLL lock; PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD; LINK_CTRL2[TGT_SPEED]; PL_LINK_CTRL[LNK_MODE]
Remarks: Verify common-clock and de-emphasis settings before transitioning to Gen2; use NUM_FTS recommendations during speed change.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions)
description: Outbound transactions map internal bus addresses into PCIe address space using 32 equally-sized regions. Region size is selectable as 1/2/4/8 MB and determines index extraction from the CPU address. Each region programs a 64-bit PCIe base via OB_OFFSET_INDEXn and OB_OFFSETn_HI, with enable per-region. Transactions that exceed region boundaries exhibit unspecified behavior and must be split by software. Translation applies to memory read/write requests, not to configuration or I/O.
evidence: Section 2.7.1 Outbound Address Translation; OB_SIZE, OB_OFFSET_INDEXn, OB_OFFSETn_HI (3.1.11, 3.1.80, 3.1.81)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]; Correct OB_SIZE; Proper region base programming
Remarks: Plan regions to avoid large bursts crossing boundaries; ensure 64-bit fields are zero when using 32-bit addressing.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions) with BAR association
description: Inbound accesses from PCIe are accepted based on BAR match and translated into internal address space using up to four regions. Each region binds to a BAR via IB_BARn and specifies a PCIe start and internal offset using IB_STARTn_LO/HI and IB_OFFSETn. BAR0 always targets application registers and is not remappable. A BAR1 special mode allows using its value directly as start address when IB_START is zero. 64-bit inbound BARs use adjacent BAR pairs for matching.
evidence: Section 2.7.2 Inbound Address Translation; IB_BARn/IB_STARTn_LO/HI/IB_OFFSETn (3.1.82–3.1.97); BAR0 exception (2.7.2.2)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]; Correct BAR programming; BAR pairing for 64-bit
Remarks: Associate regions only with Address Space 1 BARs for data; validate BAR1 special mode limitations when enabled.

feature_name: BAR mapping and BAR Mask control; BAR0 dedicated to registers
description: BAR0 is permanently mapped to application registers for both RC and EP and cannot be remapped. BAR Mask registers, overlaid on BARs when DBI_CS2 is set, allow software to program requested sizes and attributes prior to enumeration. BAR mask bits are write-only and should be verified by test patterns. BARs in EP are typically programmed by RC during enumeration; RC BAR usage is restricted by bridge base/limit filters. Correct use of BAR masks ensures predictable inbound decode windows.
evidence: Section 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception; Section 2.7.3.1 BAR Mask Registers; STATUS_COMMAND/bridge filtering (3.2.3, 3.4.10–3.4.15)
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2] to access BARx_MASK; RC base/limit windows
Remarks: Do not modify BAR masks via the link side; clear DBI_CS2 after configuration to expose standard BAR behavior to RC.

feature_name: MSI generation and reception (RC and EP)
description: The module supports 32 MSI vectors that can be generated by EPs and received by RC, and vice versa for EP reception of MSI writes into MSI_IRQ. MSI capability is controlled by MSI_CAP, address/data by MSI_LOW32/UP32 and MSI_DATA. On reception, per-group status and enable registers gate CPU-facing interrupt events. MSI writes target the MSI_IRQ register (BAR0 space on the peer) with the vector number encoded in data. MSI and legacy interrupts are mutually exclusive at function level per PCIe specification.
evidence: Section 2.14.2.2 MSI in EP; 2.14.5.2 MSI in RC; MSI_CAP/MSI_LOW32/MSI_DATA/MSI_IRQ (3.6, 3.1.16)
dependencies: MSI_CAP[MSI_EN]; Configure MSI address/data; MSIn_IRQ_ENABLE_*; IRQ_EOI for service
Remarks: Ensure ordering-sensitive software does readbacks when required; clear MSI status and write EOI before re-arming vectors.

feature_name: Legacy interrupt generation (EP) and reception (RC)
description: EP can assert and deassert legacy INTA/B/C/D messages using EP_IRQ_SET and EP_IRQ_CLR when legacy is configured. RC aggregates legacy messages, mapping them to dedicated interrupt lines and requiring software to probe downstream status. The pending condition persists until a deassert is observed, and EOI ordering races are possible. Legacy interrupts are grouped via LEGACY_X registers with enable/status/clear semantics. Choose MSI wherever possible for scalability and better ordering semantics.
evidence: Sections 2.14.2.1 and 2.14.5.1; EP_IRQ_SET/CLR/STATUS; LEGACY_* registers (3.1.17–3.1.19, 3.1.56–3.1.71)
dependencies: INT_PIN configuration; LEGACY_* enables; IRQ_EOI handling
Remarks: Work around deassert-vs-EOI race by reading downstream status before EOI; only one legacy interrupt may be pending at a time per function.

feature_name: Interrupt event mapping and EOI handling
description: Up to 14 interrupt lines are exposed to the SoC interrupt controller, covering legacy, MSI, error, and power/reset events. The mapping groups 32 MSI vectors into eight CPU events and aggregates error sources. End-of-interrupt must be signaled via IRQ_EOI with the correct event number. Software enables sources via *_ENABLE_SET and clears via *_STATUS writes of one. Proper masking reduces spurious load from aggregated sources.
evidence: Table 2-10 PCIESS Interrupt Events; IRQ_EOI (3.1.15); MSIn_* and ERR/PMRST IRQ blocks (3.1.24–3.1.79)
dependencies: Enable sets per block; IRQ_EOI event numbers; CPU interrupt router setup
Remarks: Document the CPU IRQ to PCIe event matrix and centralize EOI writes to avoid missed or retriggered interrupts.

feature_name: Device power states and ASPM link states
description: The device supports PCI power states D0/D1/D2/D3hot/D3cold with software-managed transitions and PME messaging. Link power is independently managed via ASPM L0s and L1, while L2 is not supported by the controller. Active state link power policy is configured in LINK_STAT_CTRL and capability registers. D-states correlate with permissible link states per PCIe rules. Careful coordination is required to avoid entering low-power states during active traffic.
evidence: Sections 2.15.1 and 2.15.2; LINK_STAT_CTRL[ACTIVE_LINK_PM]; Table 2-11
dependencies: PMCAP/PM_CTL_STAT; LINK_CAP/STAT_CTRL ASPM bits; system PSC for module clocks
Remarks: Disable ASPM during bring-up and performance tests; re-enable with validated latencies for your topology and common-clock situation.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER)
description: AER provides detailed logging, masking, and severity control for correctable and uncorrectable errors. Status, mask, and severity registers expose per-cause bits with write-1-to-clear semantics and sticky behavior. Header logs capture the first failing TLP header across four DWORDs. Root ports can raise system errors and interrupts based on error classes. Software must classify, log, and remediate according to severity settings and deployment policy.
evidence: Sections 2.16.2.3 and 3.8: PCIE_UNCERR, PCIE_UNCERR_MASK, PCIE_UNCERR_SVRTY, PCIE_CERR, PCIE_CERR_MASK, PCIE_ACCR, ROOT_ERR_CMD/ROOT_ERR_ST/ERR_SRC_ID
dependencies: Enable AER reporting in DEV_STAT_CTRL and ROOT_CTRL_CAP; configure masks/severities
Remarks: Store-and-forward error logs before clearing; adjust severities to align with system availability goals and avoid unnecessary fatal reactions.

feature_name: ECRC generation and checking
description: The controller can generate and check End-to-End CRC (ECRC) on TLPs when enabled. Capability and enable bits reside in PCIE_ACCR with independent control for generation and checking. ECRC is optional and requires peer support for interoperability. When unsupported by a link partner, ECRC should be disabled to prevent false errors. The First Error Pointer aids in correlating the initial uncorrectable error cause.
evidence: Section 2.16.2.3.1 ECRC; PCIE_ACCR[ECRC_GEN_EN/ECRC_CHK_EN] (3.8.8)
dependencies: AER enabled; peer device ECRC capability; consistent configuration across the path
Remarks: Disable ECRC in RC when endpoints do not support it; otherwise enable both gen/check for enhanced integrity.

feature_name: Endian modes and byte swap controls
description: Although the PCIe core is little-endian at the pins, the subsystem supports both little and big endian system operation. In big-endian mode, inbound and outbound data can be byte-swizzled on 1/2/4/8-byte boundaries as configured. The ENDIAN register selects the swap granularity for both directions. Tables document how byte lanes are reordered for each mode. Correct strobe handling and swap mode are essential for data coherence with system memory.
evidence: Section 2.10.2 Support for Endian Modes; ENDIAN register (3.1.13); Tables 2-8 and 2-9
dependencies: System endianness setting; ENDIAN[ENDIAN_MODE]; TLP FBE/LBE usage
Remarks: Validate DMA descriptors and software buffers under the chosen swap mode; prefer natural alignment to minimize reordering complexity.

feature_name: DMA and buffering via VBUSM and EDMA
description: The PCIESS exposes a 128-bit slave port for outbound initiators (e.g., EDMA) and a 128-bit master port for inbound requests originating from the link. No dedicated DMA engine exists in the block; system EDMA or CPU must drive high-throughput transfers. Example configurations show payload-based aCnt/bCnt settings for write and read paths. Bandwidth targets are roughly 85% of theoretical per-lane throughput when sized correctly. Address translation can be used to map convenient CPU addresses to PCIe windows for EDMA use.
evidence: Section 2.9 DMA Support; Examples 2-4 and 2-5
dependencies: EDMA controller; OB/IB translation regions; STATUS_COMMAND[BUS_MS] for bus mastering
Remarks: Ensure EDMA burst sizes respect 128-byte limits and alignment; throttle to avoid crossing translation windows in a single transfer.

feature_name: Transaction requirements: sizes, alignment, and byte strobes
description: Outbound internal bus transactions must be 128 bytes or smaller and aligned to 8 bytes for maximum size. Misaligned writes reduce the maximum data phase to 120 bytes and must maintain contiguous byte enables. Only incremental burst types are supported; fixed or wrap bursts can lead to bus lockups. Reads may be split but are not interleaved if max read request size is respected. Software must conform to these constraints to avoid undefined behavior.
evidence: Section 2.10.1 Transaction Requirements (2.10.1.2–2.10.1.5)
dependencies: DEV_STAT_CTRL[MAX_REQ_SZ]; TLPCFG attributes; EDMA setup
Remarks: Program MRRS and payload consistently across both ends; validate strobe patterns when using sub-dword writes.

feature_name: Initialization sequences for RC and EP
description: Bring-up requires enabling the SerDes PLL, powering the PCIe domain, programming configuration registers, and then enabling LTSSM. RC additionally performs enumeration of downstream devices and sets bridge base/limit windows. EP can temporarily respond with retry during slow initialization using APP_RETRY_EN. Successful link training is indicated by LTSSM state L0 and DEBUG0/1 signals. Software must wait recommended intervals after resets before issuing TLPs.
evidence: Sections 2.11.1.1 and 2.11.2.1 Initialization; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN, APP_RETRY_EN]; DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]
dependencies: PSC power/clock; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and lock; CMD_STATUS; timing delays per 2.13
Remarks: De-assert LTSSM only after PLL lock and configuration; in EP, clear APP_RETRY_EN when ready to accept config cycles.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock monitoring
description: The integrated SerDes multiplies a differential reference clock to the required line rate and provides recovered clocks. PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL programs loop bandwidth, multiplier (MPY), and enables the PLL, while PCIE_SERDES_STS exposes lock and lane status. Recommended MPY for 100 MHz refclkp/n is 25x for 2.5 GHz line clock used by both Gen1 and Gen2. LOCK asserts after stabilization windows and must be checked before accessing PCIESS registers. Loop bandwidth and VCO range settings should match reference clock quality and jitter profile.
evidence: Section 2.3 SerDes Configuration; Tables 2-2/2-4; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and PCIE_SERDES_STS (3.1.101/3.1.102 and 2.3)
dependencies: Stable 100 MHz (or device-specific) refclk; ENPLL; VRANGE/LB settings; SSC/common-clock considerations
Remarks: Follow kicker unlock if required by device; isolate SerDes PLL from noise and verify lock per lane before enabling LTSSM.

feature_name: PIPE and PHY loopback for validation
description: The block supports link-layer (PIPE) loopback as master or slave and PHY-level internal loopback for RC mode. PIPE loopback relies on LTSSM transitions and is intended for symbol-level test with external gear. PHY loopback uses SERDES_CFGx loopback bits and Port Force Link to bypass detect and enter Polling states. BAR and bridge base/limit settings must avoid discarding looped traffic. This enables functional validation without a link partner for bring-up and factory test.
evidence: Sections 2.12.1 and 2.12.2; SERDES_CFG0/1[TX_LOOPBACK,RX_LOOPBACK]; PL_FORCE_LINK[LINK_STATE,FORCE_LINK]; PL_LINK_CTRL[LPBK_EN]
dependencies: RC mode for PHY loopback; correct LTSSM forcing; BAR0/BAR1 windows and base/limit ranges
Remarks: Configure loopback prior to any traffic to avoid sequence number desynchronization; reset LTSSM after test to return to normal.

feature_name: Port logic controls: ACK frequency, timers, and link control
description: Port Logic registers expose knobs for link behavior including ACK frequency, L0s/L1 entry latencies, NUM_FTS, and fast-link options. PL_LINK_CTRL enables DLL, loopback, scrambler disable, and directed reset. ACK_FREQ sets the DLLP ACK cadence and common-clock NFTS values for transitions. Replay and ACK/Nak timers are adjustable via PL_ACKTIMER and SYM_NUM modifiers. These settings allow fine tuning for interoperability and performance within spec limits.
evidence: Sections 3.9.2 PL_ACKTIMER; 3.9.5 ACK_FREQ; 3.9.6 PL_LINK_CTRL; 3.9.8 SYM_NUM
dependencies: Common-clock architecture; negotiated link speed; device-specific default parameters
Remarks: Use spec-compliant defaults unless interoperability testing requires changes; document deviations and validate across partners.

feature_name: Debug visibility and LTSSM state tracing
description: DEBUG0/DEBUG1 registers expose LTSSM state encoding, training events, idle detection, and PIPE sideband activity. Appendix A maps encoded values to human-readable LTSSM states for diagnostics. Transmit K indications, SKP events, and lane reversal observation assist in board bring-up. Combined with Port Logic debug, this provides comprehensive visibility without external analyzers. Sticky behavior on several bits helps capture transient states around failures.
evidence: Sections 3.9.11 DEBUG0 and 3.9.12 DEBUG1; Appendix A LTSSM States
dependencies: Enabled link clocks; stable LTSSM; software pollers/trace logging
Remarks: Poll at a moderate rate to avoid missing pulses; log LTSSM transitions with timestamps during training/recovery analysis.

feature_name: Clocking and common-clock support
description: The PCIESS relies on a PHY-supplied differential reference clock and generates functional clocks internally. Systems may use a common reference clock across link partners, required if SSC is enabled. COMMON_CLK_CFG in LINK_STAT_CTRL declares common-clock usage to reduce training sequences. Operation of all registers depends on PLL lock; accesses before lock are not reliable. The VBUSM interface clock comes from the device PLL without user configuration.
evidence: Section 2.2 Clock Control; LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG] (3.7.6)
dependencies: Stable REFCLK per CEM spec; SSC policy; SerDes PLL lock
Remarks: If common-clock is not used, expect longer training; set COMMON_CLK_CFG accordingly and size NFTS per platform guidance.

feature_name: Reset and power-domain transitions
description: The controller supports hardware resets (POR and system reset), software-initiated hot reset, and power-domain cycling. RSTCMD[INIT_RST] requests downstream hot reset, with minimum wait times before resuming traffic. Power-domain off/on resets the module and requires re-initialization and link retraining. Sticky fields in MMRs and PM logic may survive soft resets as documented. Software must quiesce traffic and confirm buffers empty before asserting resets or gating power.
evidence: Sections 2.13 and 2.13.3; RSTCMD (3.1.7); ACT_STATUS (3.1.10)
dependencies: PSC sequencing (GOSTAT); OB/IB traffic drained; reset delays (≥100 ms) and timeout rules
Remarks: Coordinate resets with peers to avoid error storms; after power cycling, restart SerDes and LTSSM sequence from the beginning.

feature_name: Transaction filtering and vendor message limitations
description: Filtering controls allow masking or passing certain TLPs and error conditions, including completion attribute matches, ECRC errors, and vendor messages. The controller does not support vendor messaging by default, and several filters default to dropping such messages. UR behavior can be tailored for out-of-BAR, poisoned, or function-mismatch cases for test purposes. Additional mask registers control DLLP abort handling and flush requests. Use of these controls should remain within compliance and is best reserved for debug and bring-up.
evidence: SYMTIMER_FLTMASK and FLT_MASK2 (3.9.9–3.9.10); Feature list (Section 1.3: no vendor messaging)
dependencies: Appropriate mask programming; awareness of compliance impact; AER policy
Remarks: Avoid enabling vendor message acceptance in production; document any relaxed filters and re-validate interoperability.

feature_name: Address Space 0 organization and remote configuration
description: Address Space 0 provides four 4 KB windows for application registers, local config, remote config, and (RC-only) remote I/O. Remote configuration cycles are performed by programming CFG_SETUP with bus/device/function and accessing the window. The IOBASE register sets the base for RC-originated I/O cycles via the 4 KB I/O window. None of these windows support burst accesses and must be treated as non-cacheable. This structure allows RC software to manage EPs and EP peers to expose control via BAR0.
evidence: Section 2.8.1 Address Space 0; CFG_SETUP (3.1.4); IOBASE (3.1.5); Table 2-7
dependencies: Correct CFG_SETUP; RC mode for I/O window; BAR0 mapping on peers
Remarks: Gate remote accesses on link-up status; serialize configuration cycles to avoid timeouts on slow devices.

feature_name: Performance configuration: payload, MRRS, and link capabilities
description: Maximum outbound payload is 128 bytes and inbound payload 256 bytes, with remote read request size up to 256 bytes by default. Device capabilities and control registers define supported payload and request sizes and negotiated link parameters. Link capabilities advertise speed and width, and status reflects negotiated values. Properly matching MRRS and payload across the fabric reduces completion splits and improves throughput. Advertised capabilities must reflect realistic buffering to avoid flow control stalls.
evidence: Features (Section 1.3); DEVICE_CAP[MAX_PAYLD_SZ] (3.7.3); DEV_STAT_CTRL[MAX_REQ_SZ] (3.7.4); LINK_CAP/STAT (3.7.5–3.7.6)
dependencies: Endpoint capabilities; RC policy; credit availability and EDMA settings
Remarks: Tune MRRS and payload for the topology; validate performance with realistic traffic and adjust NUM_FTS and de-emphasis if needed.
